retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the key anatomic relationships in the neck. Review the indications for performing radical neck dissection, modified radical neck dissection, and selective neck dissection. Explain common complications associated with radical neck dissections. Explain the expected clinical outcomes after radical neck dissection, contrasted with modified radical and selective neck dissection. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In 2002, the American Academy of Otolaryngology-Head and Neck Surgery proposed a standardized classification system for naming the various neck dissections that are still in use today. Radical neck dissection refers to the removal of levels I-V along with the SCM, IJV, and CN XI. The modified radical neck dissection also removes levels I-V but spares at least one non-lymphatic structure (SCM, IJV, or CN XI). Selective neck dissection refers to any procedure which removes one or more levels of the neck based on patterns of cervical metastasis. Finally, an extended neck dissection refers to any neck dissection that removes additional structures of lymph nodes from areas not addressed in radical neck dissection. [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Over time, the procedure has been modified to reduce morbidity while maintaining oncologic efficacy. The modified radical neck dissection, which advocated for preserving at least one of the critical non-lymphatic structures (CNXI, IJV, or SCM), was proposed by Drs. Bocca and Suarez independently in the 1960s. [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The neck is a complex and dense anatomical area. A thorough understanding of the critical structures, fascial layers, and cervical lymph node drainage patterns in the neck is key to performing safe and oncologically sound surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Head and neck cancers most commonly metastasize initially to the cervical nodal basins. As a result, removing the cervical lymph nodes is an important component of the standard of care for many head and neck cancers. Radical neck dissection refers to removing levels I-V along with the SCM, IJV, and CN XI. The modified radical neck dissection also removes levels I-V but spares at least one non-lymphatic structure (SCM, IJV, or CN XI). Selective neck dissection refers to any procedure which removes one or more levels of the neck based on patterns of cervical metastasis. Finally, an extended neck dissection refers to any neck dissection that removes additional structures of lymph nodes from areas not addressed in radical neck dissection. This activity reviews the indications and procedural steps for radical neck dissection and briefly discusses other variants of neck dissection for head and neck cancer. This activity highlight the role of the healthcare team in managing patients with head and neck cancer.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: or a neck dissection to fall into the MRND classification, levels I-V must be removed, and at least one of the following structures must be preserved: spinal accessory nerve, sternocleidomastoid muscle, and internal jugular vein. Modified radical neck dissections are classified as type I, II, or III based on which structures are saved. In a type, I MRND CN XI is spared. In type II, MRND CN XI and the IJV are spared. In type III MRND CN XI, the IJV and the SCM are all spared. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Surgical Levels of the Neck
retrieved_docs.content: The lymph nodes in the neck are divided into seven levels by anatomic landmarks. These are based on anatomic studies delineating drainage pathways from different head and neck subsites.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Technique or Treatment -- Modified Radical Neck Dissection
retrieved_docs.content: The nodal contents are sharply divided free from the carotid sheath. Care is taken to avoid injury to the vagus nerve during the dissection. The ansa cervicalis is seen running across the IJV at this point of the dissection and may be sacrificed. Numerous IJV branches leaving anteriorly may need to be ligated during this step. The nodal tissue is elevated up to the level of the hyoid.

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Cancer Resection and Dissection  -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: 3. Modified radical neck dissection type II (MRND-II): Removal of lymph nodes from level I-V and ipsilateral sternocleidomastoid muscle, with preservation of IJV and accessory nerve. Indications for MRND-II include bulky nodal disease with SCM involvement but sparing the IJ or accessory nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Studies detailing the lymphatic drainage pathways of various head and neck regions further altered the classical radical neck dissection allowing for the dissection of limited lymph node basins of the neck based on tumor location. As anatomic and oncologic understanding has improved, neck dissection has become increasingly narrow in scope. Prophylactic neck dissections are also utilized for any clinically negative head and neck tumor with a greater than 15 to 20% chance of occult metastasis to the neck. [5] [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Neck Cancer Resection and Dissection  -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Before discussing the indications for neck dissection and the operative technique, it is essential to review the brief background, history, and types of neck dissection. The credit for neck dissection as a curative procedure for cervical metastases belongs to George Washington Crile from the Cleveland Clinic. [5] In 1900, he performed different types of neck dissections and subsequently described the classic operation of the radical neck dissection (RND) in his seminal article of 1905 published in the Transactions of the Southern Surgical and Gynecological Association. This operation is the basis of all neck dissections, with subsequent surgeries framed as modifications of this initial operation. Hayes Martin from Memorial Sloan-Kettering Cancer Center, who described the stepwise procedure of RND in his classic article in 1951, popularized this operation. However, this operative procedure is not without significant morbidity, as it results in a cosmetic deformity and dysfunction of shoulder movement due to en bloc resection of the accessory nerve, sternocleidomastoid muscle, internal jugular vein, and the tail of the parotid gland. Argentinian surgeon Oswaldo Suarez was the first to describe functional neck dissection in 1963, now called modified radical neck dissection (MRND). He described the removal of all five lymph node levels in the neck while preserving the spinal accessory nerve, sternocleidomastoid muscle, and internal jugular vein to limit any functional disability in the shoulder. [6] The types and indications of various types of neck dissection are as follows:

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Technique or Treatment -- Modified Radical Neck Dissection
retrieved_docs.content: The dissection begins with an incision of the fascia over the SCM and medial dissection in the superior third of the SCM to identify CN XI. The nerve is then skeletonized superiorly to the posterior belly of the digastric muscle and posteriorly to the trapezius. After skeletonization of the anterior border of the trapezius muscle, the fat and lymph nodes of level V are elevated and rolled medially. Cervical rootlets are often preserved, though they can also be divided while preserving the deep layer of the deep cervical fascia overlying the levator scapulae and scalene musculature to protect the phrenic nerve and brachial plexus. [17] The nodal packet is then passed under the SCM.

retrieved_docs.title: Radical Neck Dissection -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Level: VI (central compartment nodes)
retrieved_docs.content: Critical structures and relationships: The recurrent laryngeal nerve runs through level VI. The right recurrent laryngeal nerve courses more obliquely due to coursing around the innominate artery, while the left nerve has a more vertically oriented course after looping around the aorta. The thyroid and parathyroid glands are also located in level VI.

retrieved_docs.content: Oral Cavity: I-III Oropharynx: II-IV Nasopharynx: II-IV Hypopharynx: II-IV Larynx: II-IV Thyroid: VI

MRND-III is indicated in metastatic disease with limited extracapsular spread and the IJ, SCM, and accessory nerve can all be dissected free. [8] [9] Indications:

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 890
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure serves as the anatomic site associated with thyroid cancer. Thyroid cancer is classified under the broader category of thyroid disorders (اضطراب صماوي) and is clinically linked to the surgical removal of the thyroid gland. Surgical management of thyroid disease includes thyroidectomy, which may be performed as a complete removal (استئصال الدرقية) or as a partial removal (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي). Both complete and partial thyroidectomy are sub‑procedures encompassed by the general procedure termed “thyroidectomy; partial or complete.” The head and neck region is therefore the target anatomy for these thyroidectomy procedures. Additionally, partial thyroidectomy frequently co‑occurs with the anatomical reference “دراق,” reflecting its relationship to the thyroid gland itself. Understanding the connections among the head and neck anatomy, thyroid cancer, underlying thyroid disorders, and the spectrum of thyroidectomy techniques is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and patient care.

Parathyroid exploration is captured by parathyroidectomy procedures, which may be performed alone or with mediastinal exploration via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. Collectively, these diverse surgical techniques illustrate how the umbrella term “exploration procedure” integrates multiple organ‑specific operations, each with its own methodological variations and optional adjunctive steps, to facilitate diagnostic assessment, tumor removal, ductal clearance, or decompression in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: “Orchiectomy, radical, for tumor; with abdominal exploration,” and all parathyroidectomy variants with mediastinal exploration. Excision serves as the parent concept for the clinical action group “Clinical actions A‑G” and is linked to diagnostic frameworks for colorectal cancer, underscoring its relevance to oncologic tissue removal. A clear boundary separates the two categories: specific laminectomy procedures performed for spinal stenosis—e.g., “Laminectomy with exploration and/or decompression of spinal cord and/or cauda equina, without facetectomy, foraminotomy or discectomy, more than 2 vertebral segments; thoracic”—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category and remain classified solely as exploration procedures. Beyond these core domains, excision is a fundamental technique in head‑and‑neck (otolaryngologic) surgery, serving therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx. It also constitutes the operative component of percutaneous nephrolithotomy, a minimally

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1712
retrieved_docs.content: Excision serves as a broad parent category for a variety of surgical actions, including the set of procedures labeled “Clinical actions A‑G.” Within this overarching category, many specific operative methods are classified as forms of excision: cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (both open and laparoscopic), choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage or calculus removal (with or without cholecystotomy and with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty), transcranial orbital exploration with lesion removal, cervical and lumbar laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) that decompress nerve roots and may involve partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, or disc excision, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via a cervical approach or a sternal split approach. At the same time, the excision category explicitly excludes several laminectomy techniques used for spinal stenosis: thoracic laminectomy with cord or cauda equina decompression without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for one or two vertebral segments; and cervical, lumbar, or thoracic laminectomy for more than two vertebral segments performed under the same constraints. Excision is also linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, indicating that excisional techniques extend beyond deep organ surgery to include facial and upper aerodigestive tract interventions. This integrated view highlights excision as a versatile surgical concept that encompasses numerous specific operative methods while delineating clear boundaries for procedures it does not cover.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 619
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that encompasses a variety of operative methods. It includes arthroscopic removal of loose bodies from a joint, several types of cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration (including choledochoenterostomy and transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty with or without cholangiography), exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial, laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy and disc excision at a single lumbar interspace, radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration combined with mediastinal approaches such as sternal split, transthoracic access, or cervical mediastinal exploration. Excision also serves as the methodological basis for other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, as well as for percutaneous nephrolithotomy (perkutánní nefrolitotomie). In addition, Excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating its relevance to reconstructive nasal surgery. Certain extensive spinal procedures are explicitly excluded from the Excision category, namely laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy when more than two vertebral segments are involved in either the cervical or lumbar spine. The hierarchical structure places Clinical actions A‑G as a parent group under Excision, and Excision is also referenced in a colorectal‑cancer diagnostic protocol, highlighting its role in broader clinical pathways. Overall, Excision functions as a central concept that unites diverse operative techniques while delineating specific exclusions and connections to related procedural domains.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2203
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically linked to the surgical treatment known as thyroidectomy; partial or complete, a procedure that specifically targets the head and neck structure. The term thyroidectomy; partial or complete is a detailed form of the broader Arabic term استئصال الدرقية, which serves as its parent designation. This relationship underscores that thyroidectomy (partial or complete) is the primary operative intervention for managing cancer of the thyroid within the head and neck region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2167
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is a broad surgical category that falls under the parent group “Clinical actions A‑G.” It includes a wide range of operative methods such as partial or complete adrenalectomy (with or without biopsy) performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal approaches; cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, both with and without cholangiography; choledochotomy or choledochostomy with duct exploration, drainage, or calculus removal, optionally combined with transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty; transcranial exploration of the orbit with lesion removal; laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and disc excision in a single lumbar interspace; laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common‑duct exploration; radical orchiectomy for tumor with abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration performed via cervical or transthoracic approaches. The Excision category explicitly excludes certain laminectomy procedures: lumbar laminectomy with exploration and decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina that involves more than two vertebral segments, and cervical laminectomy with similar exploration that does not include facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy. In addition to these operative techniques, Excision is linked to other therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is related to plastic procedures on the nose. This integrated framework illustrates how Excision groups diverse anatomical surgeries while defining specific procedural exclusions and connections to related therapeutic domains.

Conversely, more extensive laminectomies involving multi‑segment exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina—without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the excision category. Excision further extends to breast, urologic, and endocrine procedures. Nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, radical orchiectomy for tumor removal combined with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy—whether performed with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split or transthoracic approach—are all described as excisional interventions. Beyond these, excision is linked to other therapeutic domains, serving as a method for procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it is associated with plastic procedures on the nose. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is classified as a clinical action within the broader group “Clinical actions A‑G” and serves as an umbrella term for a wide range of operative procedures. It includes adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, laparoscopically, or dorsally, with or without biopsy and with possible removal of adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—as well as several biliary surgeries such as cholecystectomy with exploration of the common duct, choledochoenterostomy, transduodenal sphincterotomy or sphincteroplasty, and associated cholangiography, which can be performed via open or laparoscopic techniques. Craniectomy or craniotomy performed exploratively in the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region also falls under Excision, as do various parathyroidectomy approaches—including cervical, sternal‑split, and transthoracic routes—each potentially combined with mediastinal exploration. In spinal surgery, Excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and excision of herniated intervertebral disc at a single lumbar level, but explicitly excludes laminectomy procedures that involve only exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy for spinal stenosis across cervical and thoracic segments (both 1‑2 vertebral levels and more extensive segments). Additionally, Excision covers “other or therapeutic procedures on nose, mouth and pharynx” and is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, highlighting its relevance to both general surgical and specialized reconstructive interventions. This comprehensive definition clarifies the scope of Excision, distinguishing it from specific laminectomy techniques while integrating diverse surgical modalities under a single procedural category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the target anatomy for the surgical procedure thyroidectomy; partial or complete, indicating that removal of the thyroid gland—whether in part or in entirety—is performed within this anatomical region. This procedure is also known by its Arabic name استئصال الدرقية, which serves as the parent term for the English description of thyroidectomy; partial or complete. Understanding that the thyroid gland resides in the head and neck area and recognizing both the English and Arabic terminology helps clinicians accurately plan and communicate surgical interventions for thyroid disease.

Additional excisional applications span endocrine surgery (parathyroidectomy with mediastinal exploration), orthopedic surgery (hand‑sheath excisions), gynecologic surgery (excision of female perineal lesions, total urethrectomy), urologic surgery (radical orchiectomy, total urethrectomy with cystostomy), and head‑and‑neck or cosmetic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx. Collectively, this structured knowledge base organizes surgical interventions by anatomical region and procedural hierarchy, linking broad operative categories to their constituent techniques, specifying exact procedural sites (adrenal gland, biliary tree, cranial compartments, spinal levels, tendon sheath of the hand, female urethra, perineum, parathyroid, etc.), and delineating relationships
